item.title: I am fully mentally finished, motivationally speaking. — 40 replies — 160.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I am fully mentally finished, motivationally speaking.

Open thread • Replies: 40 • Updated: 2026-07-13 09:53 UTC

OP

I am fully mentally finished, motivationally speaking.

I'm an ugly beta male in my mid 30s. My social life has been non-existent for my entire adult life. I missed out on any socialising during university and my 20s and afterwards. All of human society is built for normies and ugly beta males like me are seen as shit.

My job is pointless and WFH. I'm overpaid and any sideways move will lead to worse living conditions and commuting. I'm too ugly and non-normie to ever be promoted.

Culturally, my time has passed. I have recently gained the habit of pining over playing 90s and 00s video games, and then being bored to death when actually playing them. I played video games a lot in my youth, then barely any in my 20s, and a bit more in recent years. But it's all pointless.

I have produced nothing. I have no skills. I am a viewer of lives. I missed out on the web 2.0 boom, the crypto boom, every boom, every trend. The things I do, reading and going to the gym and walking, feel like procrastination. I have never taken anything to its full conclusion. I have never put sustained effort into anything.

We spent 20 days and 203 posts together in the last thread and we have yet to formulate a method that will enable us to sit at the table in the six figure club with the rest of kinsmen. We knew this wouldn’t be easy so let's not get dispassionate.

For my next proposal I will say we focus on developing a skill that will increase our value. If we all level up on our own it will only take longer. If we all level up by 2-3 points a month then cumulatively we will all have gotten stronger as a mass society. Perhaps one amongst us can elevate themselves and come back to teach the others!

How long shall we be waiting then? How long will our own potential be mocked by others? To what limit will we continue to persist in the underclass?

love your attitude op so i've come to bring you knowledge that yall may already know. i'm almost at the 6 figure table.

1. live with your parents, you losers are probably already doing this. with minimal living expenses you can invest more. also allows you to drop the emergency fund which again lets you invest more.

2. be young, youth is an advantage for compounding interest. a huge advantage.

3. get a job, yes a wage slave. obviously a better job will allow you to invest more. it won't be forever. the sooner you hit the rpg grind irl the sooner you can spend the rest of your life eating hot pockets and playing vidia.

4. invest in growth etf's. your crypto is fools gold. yes the stock market can crash but it always bounces back even if it takes years to do it. and you all are young enough to ride out the bad years. once you get old, or have enough then sell your growth etf and get dividend etfs and live the good life.

5. you can't just invest in safe etf's but you have to place them in the right location.

5a) 401k up to employer match, this is the best because its free money for you but the investments are usually not the best. if you have the option get the ROTH 401k not the normal one if the company offers it. for a normal 401k what you put in is tax differed, when you draw from it at 59 1/2 then you pay taxes on it.

5b) ROTH ira is you own personal retirement account. you pay taxes on what you put in but no taxes on what grows inside or when you pull your money out. 59 1/2 also. has a limit on how much you can invest into it each year so max it.

5c) health savings account, hsa. what you contribute can be deducted from your taxes, what grows in it not taxed and when you spend it on a medical expense its not taxed. if you save your bills you can reimburse yourself at a later date once the account grows huge. at 65 you can just withdraw from the account as if it was income.

im just some old fag trying to help my younger brothers.

This can be a positive for us as another bullrun will no doubt be happening in the next two years, so knowing in advance what some of the winning tokens could be will help us no doubt.

Knowing this we can practice making some memes and see if they catch on. This won't make us any money today or tomorrow or even next week but it will allow us to see if we are capable of producing a product that people would be willing to accept

The sharty is a perfect example of artists taking over the collective, imagine if they had sought to make there trolling profitable!

62474015
bump

62474400
>>62470191
>1
mostly agree, but you should never drop the emergency fund
even if you have a great relationship with your parents, anything can happen that changes your living situation (e.g. relationships sour, parental hardship trickles down, new job opportunity requires relocation etc.)
just keep the fund in a money market fund since the (sometimes) higher yield of hysa's often come with restrictions (i.e. daily/monthly transfer limits from the account to your checking)

>2
true even if mostly uncontrollable
staying healthy may prolong life and therefore extend working/investing "lifespan"

>3
true

>4
>growth etfs
>dividend etfs
this is basically sector/style tilt and ill-advised/suboptimal
you should invest in the broad, global market rather than being particular about if a collection of stocks are growth or value or pay dividends
there are a lot of great stocks in all groups and you shouldn't exclude them because of a selection bias
that whole "dividends are income" concept is a trap when you consider that stock price drops by the same amount as the dividend; it's basically like a forced liquidation of your shares

continued in next post

62474455
>>62470191
>>62474400
part 2:

>5
mathematically asset location (which account holds which type of investments [stocks, bonds, cash equivalents etc.]) is optimal but (and this is personal opinion) the deposit limits of different accounts can make it difficult to maintain 100% efficient asset allocation (the specific percentages of your portfolio that are in stocks, bonds etc.)
for example, let's say you have a traditional 401k ($1000 in bonds) and roth ira ($2000 in stocks) that are perfectly balanced to your desired portfolio (2/3 stocks and 1/3 bonds) and you have $1000 to invest
since you weren't sure if a bill was going to come up this year, you didn't invest this money and it is now december
while it's easy to dump the $667 in your roth ira stocks, what are you going to do about the $333 bond investment? since bonds grow slower, it's usually recommended to put them in traditional accounts to mitigate the future tax bill while you stuff your roth with stocks. but you can't just shove it into your 401k since all contributions have to be deducted from your paycheck rather than originating from your bank
in this example $333 is small compared to most paychecks (it's like an 80 hour pay period at $4.16 an hour) and therefore easy to redirect, but the same can't be said if your invest amount is significantly larger than $1000

>5a
mostly agree
i would argue opting for traditional 401k if you live paycheck to paycheck (or slightly above) since it allows you to invest a little more (although pretax) than if you invest after tax (like with a roth) and have a little more net pay than if you just accept tax on your entire gross amount (i imagine this is dependent on your state/local tax amount)

>5b
agree

continued in next post

62474470
>>62470191
>>62474400
>>62474455
part 3:

>5c
mostly true with two caveats, california and new jersey
both states treat them as regular brokerage accounts and therefore tax capital gains (gains made when you sell) and capital distributions (interest, dividends etc.)
i believe most hsa accounts don't create a tax form for you, so you have to manually go through and count what's taxable yourself
the workaround is to hold investments exempt from state tax, which is nothing but us government bonds
for me (personal opinion for rest of post) sinking funds into treasuries which will grow (long term) less than stocks isn't worth it
if you're investing, then you're probably capable of saving and having an emergency fund
that's why i would just chuck would be hsa contributions into a taxable brokerage account
this way, you won't get penalized on taking capital distributions out of the account to pay taxes on said distributions (as opposed to being penalized for withdrawing from hsa or paying the extra tax out of pocket)
yes you lose federal tax advantages, but that's irrelevant if you don't have enough to max out your roth ira each year
instead of having a little bit of money to invest and trying to decide which account to throw it in, you should get your 401k match before maxing roth ira before circling back to max 401k (possibly roth 401k) before worrying about hsa/brokerage
also, even if you don't live in ca or nj, i think those states still tax your hsa if you ever move to those states (like if chasing a new job opportunity) regardless of which state you opened the account in

fin
also i'm on mobile, so that's why my id keeps changing

62475015

Don’t
Hire

62474751
even though I'm not a neet after being laid off I feel like I'm in a similar situation.
I have a leg up with my work experience and education but I don't feel like it was worth it which meant that I wasted my youth doing something even worse than just fucking around

62474795
to all the people in this situation who want actual sane advice, here it:

Do something easy. Something that won't demand a superior work ethic or a miserable grind with a manager looking at production numbers watching you.
You're likely gonna hate your life every single morning you wake up and have to work AND you didn't do this when you were 20 years old and it was easy. You can get over the hump and become a "worker" but the mornings are already your enemy, don't stack the odds further against you.

Work behind a register. Ideally at a gas station or some sleepy business. Maybe a long shot for you to get hired, but it's ideal. Some cleaning, some dealing with dickheads, some stocking. You won't be worked to the bone. A grocery/dollar store cashier is worse in most cases.

Inbound call centre. Yes you will hate your job but the work comes at you and your office should be air conditioned. If you have superior (compared to your coworkers) manners and people skills you may even find yourself promoted rapidly. 60 year old grannies get jobs in call centers and end up trainers and managers, you can do it.

Janitor. This one may be a stretch for a 40yo NEET but if you get the right gig, or hop into the right gig after getting a year experience, you may have found a cozy job. Many janitors are not running around like rats and barely have to deal with coworkers or the public. Maybe you end up hired by the local school board in half a year and make "decent" money.


If you really want to try your hand at job where you have a taskmaster watching you try these:

Warehouse worker.
Meatpacker.
Day laborer: Construction, landscaping, whatever.
Factory as a production worker. GL getting hired unless the job is literal hell with high turnover.
Outbound Call Centre

Good luck. If you show up every day for a 1 year or 2 a manager or senior coworker will likely give you a glowing reference. You can even become a real "worker" and have easier mornings in time.

62474821
>>62474795
None of those are hiring.

62474824
>>62474821

62474838
>>62474824

62474844

Explain EVERYTHING. Learning how to utilize LLMs will really help you anyway. I'm getting close to 40 myself and I use LLMs daily (I am a professional with a good job not a NEET but it doesn't matter you need to use LLMs now even if you're employed). Anons ITT are mostly going to dunk on you because you're a huge fucking loser but the best time to turn your life around is right the fuck now. The LLM won't make you feel bad like everyone here is doing too.

Explain your interests to it too and maybe it can try and recommend a job you'll actually excel somewhat in, although beggars can't be choosy at this point.

62472755
>>62472649
5 more years :DDDDDDDDDDD

62472818
>>62461988
Everest didn’t randomly integrate Stellar — they built on it deliberately because Stellar is positioned to become the compliant public blockchain backbone for the tokenized economy, and Everest is engineering the regulated on-ramps/wallets/payments layer to capture it. Here’s the maximalist, everything-aligns thesis:
1. Strategic Positioning at the Perfect Intersection
• Everest = MiCA-licensed (EU passport), compliant custody, payments, fiat on/off-ramps, and now native Stellar wallet generation (BIP39 in EverWallet via SCF Kickstart Award).
• Stellar = Fast, cheap, compliance-native (clawbacks, authorized trustlines, etc.), with massive institutional validation via DTCC.
• The Kickstart Award isn’t just funding — it’s SDF officially stamping Everest as a key builder. This opens doors to deeper integration, more grants, co-marketing, and priority support as Stellar scales.
Everest becomes the regulated European gateway for Stellar flows: EU users/institutions access DTCC-tokenized assets (stocks, ETFs, Treasuries), stablecoins, and cross-border payments seamlessly through EverWallet.

62472822
>>62472818
DTCC connecting its tokenization service to Stellar (DTC assets live H1 2027) is one of the biggest TradFi-crypto bridges ever. We’re talking infrastructure for quadrillions in annual settlement volume potentially touching a public chain.
• Tokenized RWAs on Stellar need compliant wallets, custody, transfers, and EU distribution.
• Everest’s MiCA license + Stellar-native wallet = plug-and-play solution for European participants. No need for institutions to build everything themselves.
• Result: Explosive volume on Stellar higher XLM demand (fees, liquidity, bridges) + massive utility for Everest’s platform.
This isn’t payments-only anymore. It’s Everest sitting at the intersection of Wall Street tokenization and EU-regulated access.
3. Tokenized Economy Flywheel
• MiCA Advantage: Everest is already authorized for custody, transfers, etc. As MiCA matures and tokenized securities/stablecoins boom in Europe, Everest white-labels solutions for merchants, fintechs, and institutions.
• Wallet + Payments Moat: EverWallet with seamless Stellar (plus BTC/SOL roadmap) becomes a multi-chain compliant hub. Users generate Stellar addresses easily, hold tokenized assets, send remittances, swap, earn — all regulated.
• Network Effects: More assets on Stellar (DTCC + others) more users need compliant interfaces Everest captures fees, custody spreads, and ecosystem partnerships. SCF validation accelerates this.

62472827
>>62472822
• Stellar TVL and volume surge from DTCC flows XLM utility and price re-rate significantly (institutional adoption narrative goes parabolic).
• Everest, as an early regulated builder with SDF backing, sees user growth, revenue from services, and potential token ($ID) or valuation upside as the “Stellar-compliant layer in Europe.”
• Multi-chain expansion (already hinting BTC/SOL) positions them beyond one network while Stellar remains the core for real-world compliant assets.
• Timing: 2026–2027 window is when pilots go live. Everest’s Kickstart work is shipping now, giving them first-mover polish in wallets/UI.
Why They Chose Stellar “For a Reason”: Low costs + speed for payments/remittances, built-in compliance primitives perfect for regulated tokenized securities, and now direct DTCC pipeline. Ethereum is great for DeFi but heavier/costlier for high-volume settlement. Stellar is the efficient, institutional-friendly public chain.
In the super scenario: DTCC/Stellar becomes the standard for tokenized securities. Everest rides it as the go-to EU-compliant wallet/custody/payments layer. XLM reprices for infrastructure status. Everest scales into a major fintech player in the tokenized economy, with strong revenue, user base, and ecosystem gravity.
This is the aligned, non-random bet: regulation + infrastructure + timing + grants + institutional tailwinds. High conviction setup if the DTCC integration delivers.
